name: From Grau Roig to the bordas of Envalira — round trip
name_original: De Grau Roig a las bordas de Envalira — ida y vuelta
description: Cycle from Grau Roig to the bordas of Envalira on this moderate 7.3-mile (11.8 km) touring route through high alpine landscapes.
region_intro: Dog friendly hiking trails in the Pyrenees traverse a diverse mountain range forming a natural border between France and Spain, stretching over 430 km. The region is characterized by rugged peaks, deep glacial valleys, and extensive forests, transitioning from Atlantic-influenced landscapes in the west to Mediterranean climates in the east. Hikers can explore varied terrains including alpine meadows, granite massifs, and areas with numerous glacial lakes and waterfalls. Best dog friendly hiking trails in the Pyrenees The most popular dog friendly hiking route is Cola de Caballo via the Soaso Steps from the Ordesa Meadow, a 11.1 miles (17.8 km) trail that takes 5 hours 18 minutes to complete. This difficult route leads to the impressive Cola de Caballo waterfall within the Ordesa Valley. Another top favourite among local hikers is Ibones d'Ordicuso – semi-circular from Baños de Panticosa, a moderate 2.9 miles (4.6 km) path. This route offers views of glacial lakes (ibones) and surrounding peaks. Local hikers also love the Iron Route Trail, a 4.0 miles (6.5 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 52 minutes. Hiking in the Pyrenees is defined by dramatic peaks, glacial lakes, and dense woodlands, offering a wide range of options for different ability levels. The routes in Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 38,000 reviews. More than 340,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Pyrenees's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many dog-friendly hiking trails are available in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees offer a vast network of trails suitable for hiking with your dog. You can explore over 180 dog-friendly routes, ranging from easy strolls to more challenging mountain expeditions.

Q: Are there easy dog-friendly hikes suitable for beginners or older dogs?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees cater to all levels. There are 28 easy dog-friendly routes perfect for beginners, families, or dogs who prefer a more relaxed pace. These trails often feature gentler terrain and shorter distances.

Q: Can I find dog-friendly circular routes in the Pyrenees?
A: Absolutely. Many dog-friendly trails in the Pyrenees are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point without retracing your steps. For example, consider the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1218906465/ascent-to-the-ibones-d-ordicuso-circular-by-the-cascada-de-argualas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Ibones d'Ordicuso — circular by the Cascada de Argualas&lt;/a&gt;, which offers stunning views and a refreshing waterfall.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly trails that feature waterfalls?
A: The Pyrenees are renowned for their spectacular waterfalls. You can find dog-friendly trails that lead to impressive cascades, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10530225" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beech Forest – Cave Waterfall loop from Rio Arazas&lt;/a&gt;. The region's glacial valleys often hide beautiful waterfalls, providing a refreshing stop for you and your dog.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on dog-friendly hikes in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees offer incredibly diverse landscapes. You and your dog can explore everything from dense pine and beech forests to open alpine meadows, rugged peaks, and serene glacial valleys. The region transitions from the Atlantic coast to the Mediterranean, offering varied scenery throughout.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking with my dog in the Pyrenees?
A: The best time for dog-friendly hiking in the Pyrenees is typically from late spring to early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is generally milder, and most trails are free of snow. Summer offers lush greenery and warmer temperatures, while spring and autumn provide cooler weather and vibrant foliage.

Q: Are there specific rules or considerations for hiking with dogs in Pyrenees National Parks?
A: Yes, when hiking with your dog in protected areas like the Pyrenees National Park (France) or Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park (Spain), it's crucial to be aware of specific regulations. Dogs are often required to be on a leash, and in some sensitive zones, they might not be permitted at all to protect wildlife. Always check the specific park's rules before your visit.

Q: What do other hikers say about dog-friendly trails in the Pyrenees?
A: The dog-friendly trails in the Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars. Reviewers often praise the breathtaking natural beauty, the variety of terrain, and the opportunity to share these incredible experiences with their canine companions.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly trails that lead to lakes or offer swimming opportunities?
A: The Pyrenees are dotted with numerous glacial lakes, known as "ibones" in the Aragonese Pyrenees. Many trails lead to these beautiful bodies of water, offering a chance for your dog to cool off. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e937749845/ibones-d-ordicuso-semi-circular-from-banos-de-panticosa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibones d'Ordicuso – semi-circular from Baños de Panticosa&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that takes you to stunning mountain lakes. The famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5186545" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lac d'Oô&lt;/a&gt; is another popular spot.

Q: Are there any easy, short dog-friendly walks in the Pyrenees?
A: For a shorter, easier walk with your dog, the Pyrenees offer several options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1389836371/iron-route-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Route Trail&lt;/a&gt; is an easy hike with minimal elevation gain, perfect for a leisurely outing. These types of trails are ideal for a quick stretch or for dogs with less stamina.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions I can visit with my dog in the Pyrenees?
A: Beyond the trails themselves, the Pyrenees boast many natural wonders. While some areas like the core of Cirque de Gavarnie might have restrictions, you can often find dog-friendly paths leading to viewpoints or surrounding areas. The region is rich in glacial formations, dramatic peaks, and diverse ecosystems, offering plenty to explore with your dog.
tips: Gravel road that provides an alternative to the main road and that is taken from Les Bordes d'Envalira. It circulates on one side of the river, avoiding the stream itself.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Grau Roig, which is a ski station area. You should find parking available there, likely near the base of the ski lifts, which serves as the trailhead for this cycling tour.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This touring bicycle route is primarily on mostly paved surfaces. However, be aware that some segments might be unpaved and could be challenging for a standard touring bike. The route traverses a magnificent high alpine landscape.

faqs.question: What scenic views or landmarks can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect magnificent views through a high alpine landscape. From elevated points, there are often endless views over Andorra and the French frontier. The upper slopes of Port d'Envalira are particularly noted for their beauty. You'll also pass by the Alternative Bordes d'Envalira. However, the passage through the ski station of Pas de la Casa in summer can be less appealing.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in this high alpine region is typically outside of the peak winter season. However, be mindful that the period from July to September can see a significant volume of traffic, including heavy goods vehicles, especially on the Port d'Envalira section, which might detract from the experience.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily on roads and some unpaved sections, it's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially given the potential for traffic and the high alpine environment. Always ensure your dog is accustomed to cycling alongside you and that you carry enough water for them.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It uses public roads and trails in Andorra.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as 'moderate' with an elevation gain of around 300m over 11.8km, making it a manageable climb for many. While much of it is paved, the potential for unpaved sections and significant traffic on the Port d'Envalira, especially during peak season, means it might be less ideal for very young children or absolute beginners. Cyclists should have good fitness.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenges or things to be aware of on this route?
faqs.answer: A significant consideration is the traffic, particularly on the Port d'Envalira section, which is a major road connecting Andorra with France and Spain. The volume of traffic, including heavy goods vehicles, can be unpleasant and potentially dangerous for cyclists, especially during peak season (July to September). Consider cycling during off-peak hours or seasons to avoid the heaviest traffic.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route overlaps with sections of other known trails. Approximately 85% of it follows the official cycling route [CIMA AN09] Port d'Envalira. Smaller portions also intersect with GRP La volta a Andorra (12%) and Camí del Port Dret (6%).

faqs.question: What type of bicycle is best suited for this route?
faqs.answer: Given that it's a 'touringbicycle' route primarily on paved surfaces, a touring bike is suitable. However, since some segments might be unpaved, a gravel bike or an e-bike could offer more versatility and comfort, especially if you plan to explore any dirt track options.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restaurants near the route?
faqs.answer: The route starts near Grau Roig, which is a ski station, so you can expect to find some facilities like cafes or restaurants there, especially during the operational seasons. The area around the Bordas of Envalira might also offer options.
